The believer, perceiving that the Lord rules every heart as he sees fit, like a farmer who directs water through his fields as he pleases, seeks to have his own heart, and the hearts of others, directed in faith, fear, and love.
We are partial in judging ourselves and our actions.
Many deceive themselves with the idea that outward devotion will excuse unrighteousness.
Sin is the pride, the ambition, the glory, the joy, and the business of wicked men.
The truly diligent employ foresight as well as labor.
While people seek wealth by unlawful practices, they seek death.
Injustice will return upon the sinner, and will destroy him here and forever.
The way of mankind by nature is crooked and strange.
It is best to avoid bitter contention by pouring out your heart before God. For by prudence and patience, with constant prayer, a cross may be removed.
The evil desires of a wicked man's heart lead to baseness in his conduct.
The simple may become wise through punishments on the wicked, and through instructions to those who are willing to learn.
Good people do not envy the prosperity of wrongdoers; they see that there is a curse upon them.

Those who oppress the poor by lowering wages, those who will not relieve others according to their ability in times of distress, and those in authority who neglect to do justice, stop their ears at the cry of the poor. But care should be used in the exercise of charity.
If money can conquer the fury of passions, shall reason, the fear of God, and the command of Christ be too weak to restrain them?
There is true pleasure only in the practice of religion.

Of all wanderers in the ways of sin, those are in the most dangerous condition who turn aside into the ways of darkness. Yet there is hope even for them in the all-sufficient Savior; but let them flee to him without delay.
A life of worldly pleasure brings ruin on people.
The righteous is often delivered out of trouble, and the wicked comes in his stead, seeming as a ransom for him.
Uncontrolled passions spoil the comfort of all relationships.
The plenty obtained by prudence, industry, and frugality is desirable. But foolish people misspend what they have on their desires.
True repentance and faith will lead one who relies on the mercy of God in Christ to pursue righteousness and kindness in his conduct.
Those who have wisdom often do great things, even against those confident of their strength.
It is our greatest concern to keep our souls from being entangled and troubled.
Pride and arrogance make people passionate; such continually deal in anger, as if it were their trade to be angry.

Here is the misery of the lazy: their hands refuse to labor in an honest calling by which they might get an honest livelihood; yet their hearts do not cease to covet riches, pleasures, and honors, which cannot be obtained without labor. But the righteous and industrious have their desires satisfied.
When holiness is pretended, but wickedness intended, that especially is an abomination.
The doom of a false witness is certain.
A wicked person defies the terrors of the law and the rebukes of Providence. But a good person asks, What does God require of me?

Means are to be used, but after all, our safety and salvation are only from the Lord. In our spiritual warfare we must arm ourselves with the whole armor of God; but our strength must be in the Lord, and in the power of his might.
